_meta.source_notebook: Talkin Flag: Global Insights from the Gridiron (e70d736e-e7f2-4360-97b6-0948f10e16c9)
_meta.grounding: Every correct answer traces to a cited NotebookLM source (official rulebooks, route-tree guides, IFAF/Olympic format). EVERGREEN by design — no time-sensitive standings or 'who won last year' trivia, so the bank does not go stale.
_meta.status: DRAFT verified 2026-06-07. Owner (Ambra & Tika) must confirm each correct_index before this quiz counts toward member IQ.
_meta.scale: score_pct (0-100) is the published Flag Football IQ; Player IQ carries no poll weight.

quizzes.title: Flag Football IQ — Core
quizzes.description: Rules, strategy, route concepts, and the 5v5 / 7v7 formats. Evergreen — sourced from the Talkin Flag NotebookLM.

questions.prompt: In 5v5 flag football, how many players from each team are on the field at one time?
questions.explanation: The 5v5 format means five players per team on the field — the NFL FLAG, IFAF, and Olympic standard.

questions.prompt: In 7v7 flag football, how many players from each team are on the field at one time?
questions.explanation: 7v7 fields seven players per side; the two extra players let offenses mimic traditional tackle formations and use positional specialists.

questions.prompt: In NFL FLAG 5v5, how far behind the line of scrimmage must a defender start to rush the quarterback?
questions.explanation: A rusher must begin a minimum of 7 yards off the line of scrimmage, marked by the referee. The restriction lifts once the ball is handed off or pitched backward.

questions.prompt: Under standard 5v5 rules, how does the offense earn a first down?
questions.explanation: 5v5 usually uses a single midfield line-to-gain: four downs to cross midfield, then three downs to score.

questions.prompt: How many points is a touchdown worth in flag football?
questions.explanation: A touchdown is worth 6 points, followed by a 1- or 2-point conversion attempt.

questions.prompt: In NFL FLAG 5v5, the 1-point conversion is from the 5-yard line and the 2-point from the 10. Which is true?
choices: The 1-point try must be a pass; the 2-point may be run or pass
The 1-point may be run or pass; the 2-point must be a pass
questions.explanation: The 1-point try (from the 5) is pass-only; the 2-point try (from the 10) can be a run or a pass.

questions.prompt: What is a 'No-Run Zone' in 5v5?
choices: The end zone, where running is banned
Areas ~5 yards before the midfield line-to-gain and before each end zone where the offense must pass
Any spot within 7 yards of a rusher
questions.explanation: No-Run Zones sit ~5 yards before the midfield line-to-gain and ~5 yards before each end zone; if the ball is spotted inside one, the offense must throw. They prevent short-yardage power runs.

questions.prompt: In 5v5, can the quarterback (the player who receives the snap) run the ball across the line of scrimmage?
choices: No — the QB cannot advance the ball past the line themselves
questions.explanation: The QB cannot advance the ball across the line. The ball moves via handoffs, backward pitches, and forward passes; another player must carry it across.

questions.prompt: How many points is a safety worth, and how is it scored?
choices: 2 points; ball-carrier down in their own end zone
questions.explanation: A safety is worth 2 points when the ball-carrier is ruled down in their own end zone (flag pulled, steps out, or offensive penalty in the end zone).

questions.prompt: In NFL FLAG, how many designated rushers may a defense typically send on a play?
questions.explanation: Defenses are typically allowed up to two designated rushers per play, and rushers often must identify themselves before the snap.

questions.prompt: Once the QB hands off or pitches the ball backward, what happens to the 7-yard rush restriction?
choices: It stays for the whole play
It is eliminated — any defender may cross the line
questions.explanation: After a handoff or backward pitch, the 7-yard restriction is lifted and any defender may cross the line of scrimmage.

questions.prompt: In 5v5, which offensive players are eligible receivers?
choices: Everyone — all offensive players are eligible, including the center after the snap
Only players who started in motion
questions.explanation: Every offensive player is an eligible receiver, including the center immediately after snapping (and the QB once the ball has been handed off behind the line).

questions.prompt: What is the rule on pre-snap motion in 5v5?
choices: Only one player may be in motion, moving parallel or backward — not toward the line at the snap
Any number of players may move forward
questions.explanation: Only one offensive player may be in motion at a time, moving parallel to or backward from the line; moving toward the line at the snap is illegal.

questions.prompt: If a ball-carrier's flag falls off on its own during a run, how is the play ruled?
choices: Play continues until a two-hand touch
Down at the exact spot the flag fell
questions.explanation: If a flag falls off on its own, the runner is down at the spot the flag fell. (If a flag is missing before a catch, a one-hand touch downs them on the catch.)

questions.prompt: Which statement about being 'down' and fumbles is correct in flag football?
choices: A dropped ball is a live fumble anyone can recover
If any body part other than hands/feet touches the ground the runner is down, and a ball hitting the ground is dead (no live fumbles)
Only a flag pull can end a play
questions.explanation: A runner is down if an elbow, knee, etc. touches the ground; there are no live fumbles — once the ball hits the ground the play is dead.

questions.prompt: What is 'flag guarding'?
choices: A defender illegally covering a receiver
A ball-carrier illegally obstructing a defender's access to their flags
Guarding the end zone on defense
questions.explanation: Flag guarding is the ball-carrier illegally blocking access to their flags (arm/hand over the belt, pinning the ball, lowering a shoulder). It typically costs 5–10 yards and often a down.

questions.prompt: Which of these is NOT a form of flag guarding?
choices: Swinging an arm over the flag belt
Catching the ball with two hands away from the body
Slapping away a defender's hand as they reach for the belt
questions.explanation: Stiff-arming, swinging an arm over the belt, and slapping a defender's hand away from the belt are all flag guarding. Simply catching with two hands is legal.

questions.prompt: On defense, what must a player do to stop the ball-carrier?
choices: Strip or punch the ball loose
Pull a flag — stripping/punching the ball is prohibited
Tackle the runner to the ground
Hold the jersey until help arrives
questions.explanation: Defenders must pull a flag; stripping, punching, or knocking the ball out is prohibited, as is tackling, holding, or pushing through the runner.

questions.prompt: During a flag-pull attempt, which contact is an immediate personal foul?
choices: Incidental contact with the runner's body
Contact with the ball-carrier's head, neck, or face
questions.explanation: Incidental contact is generally allowed, but any contact with the head, neck, or face — or bull-rushing/leading with a shoulder — is a personal foul.

questions.prompt: Why does 5v5 rely heavily on quick timing throws, short routes, and option routes?
choices: Because passing is the only legal play
Because the tight spacing and fast rush demand a quick release
Because deep passes are illegal in 5v5
Because there are no defenders downfield
questions.explanation: The compact field and rush count make 5v5 a fast game built on quick timing, short routes, and option routes.

questions.prompt: In man-to-man defense, each defender is responsible for…
choices: A specific area of the field
questions.explanation: Man coverage assigns each defender to a specific offensive player; zone assigns each defender an area.

questions.prompt: In zone defense, each defender is responsible for…
choices: A specific area of the field, covering anyone who enters it
questions.explanation: Zone assigns each defender an area and they cover any receiver who enters it — strong on deep balls, weaker on short throws into the gaps.

questions.prompt: Which best describes Cover 1?
choices: A pure zone with three deep defenders
Man coverage with one deep safety (and a rusher)
Two deep safeties splitting the field
An all-out blitz with no deep help
questions.explanation: Cover 1 pairs defenders man-to-man with a single deep safety over the top, usually with one rusher.

questions.prompt: Which best describes Cover 2?
choices: A zone with two deep safeties each covering half the deep field
questions.explanation: Cover 2 is a zone with two deep safeties splitting the deep field; it can be vulnerable to short throws over the middle.

questions.prompt: Which best describes Cover 3?
choices: Three deep defenders each guarding a third, with no rusher
questions.explanation: Cover 3 is a pure zone — three deep defenders each guard a third and no one rushes, blanketing the deep field against long passes.

questions.prompt: A common weakness of zone defense is…
choices: Quick, short passes into the gaps between zones
questions.explanation: Zone keeps eyes on the QB and limits deep shots but is vulnerable to quick, short throws into the seams between zones.

questions.prompt: On the route tree, a 'slant' is best described as…
choices: A few hard steps then a quick cut inside at ~45°
A sharp 90° cut to the sideline
questions.explanation: A slant takes 1–3 hard steps, plants the outside foot, and cuts inside at ~45° for a quick, leverage-based throw.

questions.prompt: A 'go' route (also fly / streak / fade) is…
choices: A straight, full-speed vertical sprint downfield
A quick out to the sideline
A curl back to the QB
questions.explanation: A go/fly/streak is a straight vertical sprint built for fast receivers to win deep.

questions.prompt: A 'post' route breaks…
choices: Vertically, then inside at ~45° toward the middle (the goal post)
Back toward the line of scrimmage
questions.explanation: A post runs vertical then cuts inside at ~45° toward the deep middle; a corner/flag route is its mirror, breaking outside toward the pylon.

questions.prompt: What distinguishes an 'out' route from an 'in/dig' route?
choices: An out breaks toward the sideline; an in/dig breaks toward the middle
An out is vertical; an in/dig is a sprint
An out is a run play
questions.explanation: Both run forward then make a sharp ~90° cut — an out breaks toward the nearest sideline, an in/dig breaks toward the center of the field.

questions.prompt: In a numbered route tree, which is generally true?
choices: Even numbers are outside-breaking, odd are inside-breaking
Even numbers are inside-breaking, odd are outside-breaking
questions.explanation: By common convention, even-numbered routes break inside and odd-numbered routes break outside (with 9 = the vertical go).

questions.prompt: How does the run game differ between 5v5 and 7v7?
choices: 5v5 has limited running (No-Run Zones, QB can't run); 7v7 has an active run game with a dedicated running back
questions.explanation: 7v7's extra players support traditional formations and an active run game with a dedicated back, while 5v5's running is more restricted.

questions.prompt: How is the field marked for first downs in 7v7 compared to 5v5?
choices: Both use a single midfield line
7v7 splits the longer field into multiple zone lines-to-gain (often ~20-yard intervals); 5v5 typically uses a single midfield line
questions.explanation: On its larger field, 7v7 uses multiple zone lines (commonly every ~20 yards) with four downs to reach the next one; 5v5 usually uses one midfield line-to-gain.

questions.prompt: Which is a key strategic advantage of the 7v7 format over 5v5?
choices: Two extra players allow positional specialists and traditional tackle-style formations
questions.explanation: 7v7's extra players enable dedicated specialists (slot receiver, hybrid linebacker, deep safety) and more complex layered concepts.

questions.prompt: How does blocking differ between 5v5 and 7v7?
choices: 5v5 allows full contact blocking; 7v7 bans it
5v5 strictly prohibits contact blocking; some 7v7 leagues allow 'soft-screen' / shield blocking near the line
questions.explanation: Contact blocking is strictly prohibited in 5v5, while some 7v7 leagues permit limited soft-screen/shield blocking and more varied rush rules.

questions.prompt: Typical 5v5 field dimensions (NFL FLAG / youth standard) are about…
choices: 70 yards total (50-yard field of play) by 25–30 wide, with two 10-yard end zones
40 yards by 40 yards, no end zones
questions.explanation: Standard 5v5 fields run ~70 yards total (a 50-yard field of play) and 25–30 yards wide with two 10-yard end zones. IFAF/Olympic uses 50×25 with 10-yard (8 m) end zones.

questions.prompt: Compared with 5v5, a standard 7v7 field is…
choices: Larger — often 80–100 yards long and up to 40 yards wide
questions.explanation: 7v7 uses a much larger canvas (often 80–100 yards long, up to 40 wide) to support deeper, more complex route concepts.

questions.prompt: At which Olympic Games does flag football make its debut?
questions.explanation: Flag football debuts at the 2028 Summer Olympics in Los Angeles, played 5-on-5.

questions.prompt: What format will Olympic flag football use?
questions.explanation: The Olympic tournament is 5-on-5 on a 50-yard field, with six men's and six women's teams of 10-player rosters.

questions.prompt: What is the rule on active NFL players competing in Olympic flag football?
choices: Owners voted to allow them, but they must go through tryouts/qualification
They are automatically rostered for Team USA
Only retired NFL players may play
questions.explanation: NFL owners voted unanimously to permit active players, but any player must earn a roster spot through a formal tryout/qualification process run by each nation.
